SpaceX (Space Exploration Technologies Corporation) was founded by entrepreneur Elon Musk in 2002, with the goal of reducing the cost of spaceflight and ultimately enabling human missions to Mars.[1][2]

Falcon 1 was SpaceX’s first orbital launch vehicle, a small, two‑stage, liquid‑fueled rocket designed to place small satellites into orbit at lower cost than existing options.[1] Developed largely with internal funding, Falcon 1 represented the company’s initial attempt to prove it could design, build, and launch an orbital‑class rocket.

Key milestones of the Falcon 1 program

1. Program inception and purpose
- Early 2000s: SpaceX began development of Falcon 1 as its first rocket, aiming to demonstrate a privately developed, liquid‑fueled orbital launch vehicle and to offer lower‑cost access to orbit for small payloads.[1][2]

2. First launch attempt – failure (March 2006)
- March 2006: Falcon 1’s first launch attempt failed. According to Britannica, the failure was caused by a fuel leak and fire in the first stage, preventing the rocket from reaching orbit.[1]
- This flight nonetheless demonstrated that SpaceX could design and build a rocket capable of liftoff and partial flight, providing critical data for subsequent attempts.[1]

3. Second and third launch attempts – continued failures (2007–August 2008)
- 2007 and August 2008: Falcon 1’s second and third launches also failed to reach orbit.[1][2]
- These consecutive failures brought SpaceX close to financial collapse, as the company had invested much of its available capital into the Falcon 1 program.[2]

4. First successful orbital launch – historic achievement (September 28, 2008)
- September 28, 2008: On its fourth flight, Falcon 1 successfully reached Earth orbit.[1][2]
- This mission made Falcon 1 the first privately developed, liquid‑fueled rocket to reach orbit, a major milestone both for SpaceX and for commercial spaceflight more broadly.[1][2]

5. NASA contract and transition to Falcon 9 (late 2008–2009)
- Within months of the successful fourth flight, SpaceX secured a NASA contract worth more than $1 billion to provide cargo resupply services to the International Space Station.[1]
- This success effectively closed the main chapter of the Falcon 1 program, as SpaceX shifted its focus and resources to developing the larger, more capable Falcon 9 rocket.[1][2]
- Falcon 1 flew only one additional successful mission (its fifth launch) in 2009 before being retired.[2]

In summary: Elon Musk founded SpaceX in 2002. Falcon 1, the company’s first rocket, went through three failed launch attempts between 2006 and 2008 before achieving a historic first orbital success on September 28, 2008, becoming the first privately developed liquid‑fueled rocket to reach orbit. That success led directly to a major NASA ISS resupply contract and allowed SpaceX to pivot from Falcon 1 to the larger Falcon 9, which became the company’s primary launch vehicle.[1][2]

## Sources
[1] SpaceX | Spacecraft, Rockets, xAI Acquisition, & 2026 IPO – Britannica — https://www.britannica.com/money/SpaceX
[2] SpaceX – Wikipedia — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SpaceX
